system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 3: Module 3 - Let's Create a Chord Melody Arrangement (First Steps & Harmony Analysis)
Pages 89-111 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Module 3 - Let's
Create a Chord
    Melody
 Arrangement!


Chord Melody Arrangement First Steps

Good news:

We have all the ingredients we need to make a tasty chord melody arrangement.

Throughout this module, I’m going to show you an example of the process using the classic jazz
standard Fly Me To The Moon.

Here’s a video of what we’ll end up with by the end of this module:




Let’s begin this module by talking about a few preliminary steps you should have in place for you to
make an effective arrangement.


Finding a Suitable Tune to Arrange for Your First Project

Although you can turn nearly any tune as a chord melody guitar arrangement, it’s helpful to find a
chart with a melody that isn’t too fast-moving.

For beginners, ballads or swing/broadway standards usually work well.

Here is a list of tunes that are suitable for your first arranging project:

   All the Things You Are
   On Green Dolphin Street
   Have You Met Miss Jones
   Alone Together
   Autumn Leaves
   Don’t Get Around Much Anymore
   Fly Me To the Moon
   Georgia on My Mind
   Just Friends
   Moon River
   My Funny Valentine
   Night and Day



As mentioned above, I’ve chosen Fly Me To The Moon as the tune we will arrange for this module.


Listen to Recordings

Getting a feel for the melody and harmony of a tune is very important before you embark on an
arrangement – and listening to a variety of recordings is a great way to get the sound of the song in
your ears.

Here are a couple of standout performances of Fly Me To The Moon we can use as a general
reference:




Ray Brown, Monty Alexander, and Russell Malone




The groove of this trio is just sublime.

Notice guitarist Russell Malone’s cool chord walking technique in his comping in this rendition, a
perfect complement to Ray Brown’s sophisticated bassline techniques.


Vince Guaraldi




Excellent rendition by pianist Vince Guaraldi and his trio. This one kicks off with a lyrical solo piano
section before moving into full swing with the rest of the band.


Tip - Pencil & Paper vs Software

For writing out your arrangement, there’s a wide range of options when it comes to software: Guitar
Pro, Sibelius, Finale, Musescore – the list goes on.

However, the cheapest and most straightforward method is good ol’ fashioned pencil and paper.

Although it can take a little longer with this method, you will train your ears much more compared to
relying on software.

But:

Feel free to use software if you’re already familiar with a software package, or if you’re keen to give
software a try. If you’re in this boat, I can highly recommend Guitar Pro as a solid choice (and an
affordable one at that).

Whatever method you choose, it’s important to write down your arrangement somehow. Writing gives
you the ability to keep track of your ideas and thus refine them more easily (which can be difficult to
do if you are arranging entirely by ear).


Choosing a Key

Let’s begin to design our arrangement of Fly Me To The Moon.

Here’s an excerpt of the chart in the key of F Major:




   Play audio example 3-1




As mentioned in Module 1, choosing a suitable range and key is one of the most important factors in
successful guitar arranging.

At the moment, the melody is generally too low for an effective guitar arrangement (it could work but
we would need to do some very clever arranging to work around the challenges this presents).


Let’s see what it looks like if we push the melody up the octave:




   Play audio example 3-2




This register would work better for an arrangement, but I’m now facing the opposite problem – it
seems a bit too high on the fretboard.


So, instead of F Major, let’s try changing the key to C Major instead:




   Play audio example 3-3




Now we’re cooking!

Now the register and key is in a good spot on the fretboard for when we go to add chord voicings
underneath the melody notes later on.


Learn the Melody on the Top 2 (or 3) Strings

In order to fit our chord voicings underneath the melody notes, we need to change the fingering of
the melody to sit on the top 2 (or 3) strings.

Note: it’s ok to occasionally dip down the 3rd or even the 4th string if necessary – don’t get too rigid
about only sticking to 2 strings!

Let’s do this now with our chart of Fly Me To The Moon.

Here’s the melody re-fingered to sit mainly on the top 2 strings:


Excellent – we’ve now created an effective ‘canvas’ for our chord melody arrangement, which we can
add chord voicings to in the following lessons.

Before we push ahead with the arrangement though, there are a couple of extra steps we need to do
so we thoroughly understand the harmony, which will greatly simplify our arranging process.


Learn the Chords

Creating an effective chord melody arrangement is largely about being familiar with a tune. Many of
the best arranging decisions come from intuition, rather than a theoretical approach.

So:

Something good to do before getting into the nitty-gritty of what voicings to use is to simply learn
the basic chords of the song as if comping in a band.

This is another way to train your ears to the chord progression and is a gentle way to dip your toes
into applying chords to your chosen tune.


Chord Exercise #1 – Basic Comping

Although these voicings won’t (directly) be used in our arrangement, it’s still helpful to comp the
chords using basic ‘garden variety’ voicings you may already be familiar with.

Here’s an example of this on Fly Me To The Moon using a basic Charleston rhythm pattern.



 Task: watch and listen to the following comping exercise, then either play along with it using the
 notation provided OR create a similar exercise for your chosen tune.

Analyze the Harmony

Our next step in our arranging journey is to gain an understanding of how the chord progression
works for our chosen jazz standard.

We are aiming to get 3 outcomes from this:

1. To work out the quality of each chord in the progression (major, minor, dominant).
2. To observe any key changes in the tune.
3. To see if we can spot common chord sequences in the progression, such as ii – V – Is etc.




Analysis Conventions

Harmonic analysis uses the following conventions:

   Roman numbers are designated to indicate a chord’s function.
   Upper case Roman numerals indicate a chord being major or dominant.
   Lower case Roman numerals indicate a chord being minor.


Fly Me To The Moon Analysis

Check out the following analysis of the first section of Fly Me To The Moon

Some helpful observations about its chord progression:

   The progression consists of many VI ii V I progressions, both major and minor, as well as straight
   ii V I progressions.
   Similar to the jazz standard Autumn Leaves, the progression moves between relative major and
   minor keys, C and Am (relative keys are those which share the same key signature)
   The VI chord in bar 5 is used as a pivot chord to move between C Major and A Minor. It is a IV
   chord in C major but also a VI chord in A minor – a common chord between both keys.



These are all helpful observations about the jazz standard we are arranging.

Spotting common chord progressions like a ii V I and the main key changes gives you a greater
understanding of the harmony and is a crucial ingredient for an effective end result in our chord
melody arranging journey.


Analyze Intervals in the Harmony

Trust me, we are getting closer to creating our arrangement!

Just like painting the inside of a house, a lot of time goes into the preparation – if everything is well
prepared and organized the job goes much more smoothly.

In this lesson, we are going to turn our attention to the melody of the tune we are arranging.

The reason:

If we know what interval a melody note is in relation to a given chord, we will be able to find an
appropriate voicing from our chord dictionary with that interval as the top note chord.




 Important Note

 The interval number we give a melody note must relate to the current chord in the
 progression – NOT the overall key of the progression. This is a common mistake beginner
 arrangers often make.

 The interval numbers are the chord tone numbers, and could be one of the following:

 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5,b13, 13, b7, 7

 Often in jazz melodies, the chromatic notes in a melody can be considered passing tones if they
 are not on a strong downbeat and simply lead to a stronger chord tone, so you can use the label
 PT for those.


Let’s take the first melody note – the C note in the melody with Am7 in the chord progression:




To find the correct interval number, you always refer to the major scale of the current chord.

This might seem a bit confusing – but it’s the convention that’s evolved in jazz theory.


Our current chord is Am7, with A as the root note of that chord.

If we look at an A major scale:




We see that C# is 3, a ‘major 3rd’.

So, our C natural in the melody would be a b3, or ‘minor 3rd’ – the interval one half step below a
major 3rd.



 Important: Notice how this label relates to the chord in the progression at the time.


Let’s imagine I had Fmaj7 instead in the first bar of the progression:




That C on the first beat would now be a 5 in relation to an Fmaj7 chord (the 5th note out of an F
Major scale), whereas with Am7, it is a b3.



The golden rule:

 Always relate the interval to the chord at that point in the chord progression.


Fly Me to the Moon Melodic Analysis

Carefully going through the progression in this way, we can label the intervals in the melody in
relation to the chords in the progression as follows (the intervals are indicated by the numbers
between the notation and TAB staff):




Note: Sometimes a melody note anticipates the next chord so is considered an interval of that
chord (see the ends of bar 1, 3, 5, 7).

Good work! We now have all our ingredients in order to be able to confidently apply chord voicings to
the melody – head to the next lesson to get our arrangement started.


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
